one_line_takeaway: ForgeryVCR shows that letting an MLLM invoke forensic tools (ELA/noise-fingerprint/FFT/zoom-in) and reason directly over the resulting visual maps -- rather than describing tampering evidence in text -- substantially improves both image forgery detection (F1 0.8271) and pixel-level localization (IoU 0.5306) versus text-centric MLLM and specialist baselines, but the paper is a multimedia-forensics/CV contribution, not an AI-security paper, and does not address adversarial evasion of its own toolbox.
key_finding: ForgeryVCR achieves a weighted-average detection F1 of 0.8271 / Accuracy of 0.8261 across the 8 generalization benchmarks (Table 1), an absolute improvement of roughly 11 points of accuracy over the next-best method, FakeShield (F1 0.7172 / ACC 0.6891), with especially large margins on Coverage and NIST16. For pixel-level localization (Table 2) it reaches a weighted-average IoU of 0.5306 / F1 0.5881, ahead of the best traditional specialist, TruFor (IoU 0.4761), and far ahead of forensic MLLMs FakeShield (IoU 0.4047) and SIDA (IoU 0.2790). The pure Visual-Centric variant (ForgeryVCR) consistently beats its own visual+textual-CoT ablation (ForgeryVCR*: F1 0.7756/ACC 0.7711 detection, F1 0.5805/IoU 0.5249 localization), supporting the central claim that textual rationales dilute forensic precision. Ablations show: (a) RL is the single largest contributor to the final numbers (Table 4: Base Model 0.5200/0.6600 det. F1/ACC and 0.2193/0.2742 loc. F1/IoU climbs only to 0.6903/0.6893 and 0.4464/0.4049 after SFT+ISR+MTS, before RL pushes to 0.8271/0.8261 and 0.5881/0.5306); (b) all three reward terms are needed, with classification-only reward giving F1 0.7891/ACC 0.7944 but weaker localization (F1 0.5227/IoU 0.4688), and adding Rloc without Rtool improves localization but hurts detection accuracy (Table 6); (c) forensic tools and the Zoom-In tool are complementary -- either alone underperforms the combination (Table 7); (d) general MLLMs fine-tuned on the identical pipeline but without visual-centric tool use substantially trail ForgeryVCR (best among them, Qwen-3-VL-4B, reaches only F1 0.7351/ACC 0.7561 detection and F1 0.4814/IoU 0.4357 localization vs. ForgeryVCR's 0.8271/0.8261 and 0.5881/0.5306), isolating the toolbox/RL contribution from raw model scale or architecture. The paper also reports (per Table 9, referenced but not read in full detail here) that ForgeryVCR uses a substantially smaller training-data footprint than competing SOTA methods.
core_contribution: ForgeryVCR is a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) framework for Image Forgery Detection and Localization (IFDL) that reformulates the model as a proactive agent performing 'Visual-Centric Reasoning' rather than text-centric Chain-of-Thought. Instead of describing tampering evidence in words (which the authors show induces hallucinations), the model dynamically invokes a 'Hybrid Forensics Toolbox' -- Error Level Analysis (ELA), Noise Print++ (camera noise fingerprints), Fast Fourier Transform (frequency-domain artifacts), and a Zoom-In visual refinement tool -- to convert imperceptible statistical inconsistencies directly into visual intermediates that are re-injected into the model's context, and then predicts a bounding box that is fed to Segment Anything Model 2 (SAM2) to produce the final segmentation mask. Training is a two-stage 'Strategic Tool Learning' pipeline: (1) SFT cold start on trajectories built via Gain-Driven Tool Selection (only tools that measurably beat a tool-free baseline, quantified via localization IoU for manipulated images or ground-truth class probability for authentic ones, are kept) and Multi-Trajectories Synthesis (constructing no-tool, single-tool, and multi-tool reasoning paths); and (2) RL via GRPO with a composite reward combining classification reward, localization (IoU) reward, and a novel Tool Utility Reward that only pays out when a tool invocation actually improves classification or localization, discouraging indiscriminate tool calls.
threat_model: Not applicable -- this is a computer-vision / multimedia-forensics paper about detecting and localizing image tampering (splicing, copy-move, removal/inpainting, AIGC-based inpainting), not an AI-security paper. It does not model an adversary attacking the ML system itself (no adversarial examples, no attacks on the MLLM's own robustness/safety, no jailbreak or prompt-injection threat model). The 'adversary' implicit in the task is whoever forged the input image, and the paper's own robustness evaluation is limited to non-adversarial post-processing degradations (e.g., compression/noise), not adaptive attacks against the detector.
limitations: The paper's own text does not include a dedicated self-critical 'Limitations' section; the only explicit limitations discussion in the main body critiques prior work (specialist networks as black boxes; MLLM approaches as overly text-centric), which is Related Work framing, not the authors' self-assessment of ForgeryVCR. From what is directly observable in the results: localization performance is uneven across benchmarks -- IoU on Korus (0.2336) and CocoGlide (0.3785) remains well below the weighted average (0.5306), and on Korus ForgeryVCR is not the best performer either (TruFor: IoU 0.3016 vs. ForgeryVCR's 0.2336, so ForgeryVCR actually underperforms TruFor there). The evaluation protocol borrowed from FakeShield binarizes continuous outputs at a fixed 0.5 threshold for traditional networks, which may disadvantage well-calibrated probabilistic baselines. Robustness claims are evaluated against post-processing degradations, not adversarial or adaptive manipulation attacks designed to evade the specific forensic toolbox (ELA/NPP/FFT), so generalization to an adversary aware of the detection pipeline is untested in the excerpted portion of the paper.

datasets_used: CASIA v2 (SFT training, balanced subset, 5,123 authentic images per Appendix B)
quantitative_results: TruFor (best specialist localization baseline): F1 0.5467 / IoU 0.4761 (Table 2)
ForgeryVCR* (visual+textual CoT ablation): detection F1 0.7756/ACC 0.7711; localization F1 0.5805/IoU 0.5249
Best general MLLM baseline fine-tuned on same pipeline, Qwen-3-VL-4B: detection F1 0.7351/ACC 0.7561, localization F1 0.4814/IoU 0.4357 (Table 3)
